name: Nidhog (dragon)
shortDescription: Nidhog is a monstrous dragon or serpent in Norse mythology who gnaws at the roots of Yggdrasil, the world tree, and torments the souls of the wicked in Náströnd.
description: Nidhog, whose name translates to 'corpse-tearer' or 'striker of malice,' is a fearsome dragon or serpent residing in the deepest roots of Yggdrasil, the cosmic world tree. His primary role involves constantly gnawing at these roots, threatening the stability of the entire cosmos. Nidhog is a symbol of destruction, decay, and the negative aspects of existence. He dwells in Náströnd, the 'shore of corpses,' a hall in Niflheim where he torments the souls of oathbreakers, murderers, and adulterers. The souls are subjected to his venomous bites and incessant gnawing. Nidhog's existence is intertwined with the squirrel Ratatosk, who carries messages between him and the eagle perched atop Yggdrasil, often stirring up conflict between the two. While not directly involved in the major Aesir-Vanir conflicts or Ragnarok prophecies in a combatant role, his continued undermining of Yggdrasil contributes to the eventual collapse of the world. He represents the ever-present threat of chaos and the inevitable cycle of destruction and renewal in Norse cosmology. He is a primordial force of destruction, a constant reminder of the dark underbelly of existence that underlies even the most glorious aspects of the Norse world. The cyclical nature of his actions, constantly gnawing and being fed by the venom of the underworld, emphasizes the interconnectedness of creation and destruction in Norse thought.

sources.title: Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs
sources.author: John Lindow
